description:
Parse `@spec name(T1, T2, ...) :: R` declarations and attach them to the following fn/defmacro in the AST.

## Syntax

```
@spec add1(integer) :: integer
fn add1(n) do n + 1 end

@spec lookup(id, [id]) :: id | nil
fn lookup(x, xs) do ... end
```

## Mechanics

1. Parser: `@spec` recognized in the attribute position above a fn/defmacro. Body parsed as `Name ( TypeExprList ) :: TypeExpr` using .31.1.
2. AST: produces Attribute::Spec(SpecDecl { name, params: Vec<Descr>, result: Descr }) attached to FnDef.attrs via .31.2.
3. Resolution: type names in the spec body resolve against the enclosing module's ModuleTypeEnv (.31.3).
4. Parse-time arity check: SpecDecl.params.len() must equal the following fn's parameter count. Name in the @spec must match the following fn's name. Errors raised at parse time with clean diagnostics.
5. Multiple @spec on one fn: pick a rule. Probably reject for v1 (later: a fn can have multiple specs forming a union — defer). Document the choice.

## Tests

- Valid: @spec add1(integer) :: integer above fn add1(n) parses, AST carries SpecDecl
- Arity mismatch: @spec add1(integer, integer) :: integer above fn add1(n) errors
- Name mismatch: @spec other(integer) :: integer above fn add1(n) errors
- Unknown type name: @spec add1(unknown) :: integer errors (via .31.3 resolution)
- Spec without following fn: errors
- Multiple @spec on one fn: errors with a clear message

## Out of scope

- Multi-clause specs (union of arrows) — deferred
- @callback (for protocols) — separate epic
- Validation against inferred types — that is .31.5
title: @spec parser + AST attachment
